jokes.text: The opening rating game — Abbi thinks they're rating hotness, Ilana is rating dick size through basketball shorts
jokes.setup: Two women are casually rating men at what sounds like a standard hotness ranking game
jokes.punchline: Ilana has been rating visible dick sizes through b-ball shorts the entire time, operating on completely different logic that she finds self-evident
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in cold-open misdirection. The show plants a completely coherent parallel conversation that only reveals its split nature at the reveal. 'Why would we be rating them on dick size?' / 'Abbi, why wouldn't we be rating them on dick size?' is a perfect character-defining exchange — Ilana's logic is unassailable to her. The mismatched rating scores (a 10 drawing 'eww') get funnier in retrospect. Earns the 8+ because the structure is genuinely clever and only Ilana could anchor this premise.

jokes.text: 'If you train your eyes, you can see their religion.'
jokes.setup: The dick-size rating game has just been revealed; Ilana is now speaking in a confessional/talking head
jokes.punchline: Ilana delivers this as solemn life wisdom, framing ogling men's genitals through shorts as a refined spiritual skill
jokes.explanation: Seven words that are quotable for life. The economy here is exceptional — 'their religion' is doing enormous work, simultaneously a euphemism, a joke about circumcision, and a testament to Ilana's self-mythologizing. The deadpan confessional delivery frames it as hard-won expertise. This is Broad City at its sharpest: raunchy premise elevated by precision language. Justifies the 8.5 craft score on economy and character integration alone.

jokes.text: A gym employee tells the women their staring is making guys uncomfortable
jokes.setup: Women are openly ogling men's crotches at the gym
jokes.punchline: A gym employee delivers the classic 'your staring is making people uncomfortable' line — the exact complaint women receive constantly, now reversed onto men being leered at
jokes.explanation: The gender-flip on the 'your staring is making people uncomfortable' complaint is a clean, earned joke — it completes the opening bit with a social commentary button. Not particularly original as a concept (gender-role reversal humor is well-worn) but executed with the right deadpan brevity. Functional payoff to the cold open.

jokes.text: 'I've never even worked at a restaurant this nice.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says she's never been to a restaurant this nice
jokes.punchline: Abbi one-ups the sentiment by noting her frame of reference is working in restaurants — she's been to plenty she's never eaten at as a customer
jokes.explanation: A tight, economical character joke that immediately establishes both women's class position and Abbi's life specifically. The joke works on the gap between 'been somewhere' and 'eaten there' — a very real distinction for service workers. Character-specific, well-timed, punches above its weight for a throwaway line.

jokes.text: Ilana has fasted for 36 hours to 'raise her market value' by eating expensive food, and has been doing laxatives all week to make room
jokes.setup: The women are excited about the fancy birthday dinner; Ilana mentions she hasn't eaten to prepare
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'fasting 36 hours' to 'using laxatives all week' to 'my underwear is filled—' before the maître d' interrupts is a three-stage escalation ending on the most horrifying possible note, perfectly cut off
jokes.explanation: The structure here is impeccable — three escalating beats of increasingly disturbing body-prep logic, each more unhinged than the last, building to 'My underwear is filled—' which is then perfectly interrupted by the maître d's 'Good evening, mademoiselles.' The interruption IS the punchline. The juxtaposition of 'raise my market value' (aspirational economic framing) applied to eating expensive food is also a sharp Ilana-specific worldview joke. Earns 8.3 on structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi calls her dad her 'father figure' and corrects herself mid-sentence
jokes.setup: Abbi is speaking formally to the maître d' about the reservation
jokes.punchline: She starts to say 'my dad' then self-consciously upgrades to 'my father figure' — suggesting some complicated or aspirational relationship dynamic being performed for the fancy setting
jokes.explanation: A small, understated character beat — Abbi's social performance anxiety manifesting as an absurd mid-sentence correction. It's quick and subtle, mostly earning its laugh from the specificity of 'father figure' as an upgrade. Not a standout moment but does character work efficiently.

jokes.text: The coat check moment — waiter suggests Ilana may want to keep her jacket because she looks cold; she insists 'I'm perfect. Loving the temp.'
jokes.setup: Ilana hands over her coat; the maître d' diplomatically suggests she might be cold (implying her outfit is barely-there)
jokes.punchline: 'I'm perfect. Loving the temp.' — Ilana's cheerful obliviousness/defiance about clearly being underdressed or near-naked for a fancy restaurant
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that reads cleanly even in transcript — the maître d's diplomatic 'it looks like you may be cold' is clearly a polite signal about inappropriate dress, and Ilana's breezy 'Loving the temp' is a perfect non-acknowledgment. Economy of her response is strong. Character-specific but not the sharpest version of this type of joke.

jokes.text: Asking to sit at Beyoncé and Jay-Z's specific table
jokes.setup: They are being seated at a very fancy restaurant
jokes.punchline: Ilana asks to be seated at the exact table where Beyoncé and Jay-Z sat, the maître d' responds 'of course you would' with polished condescension
jokes.explanation: The maître d's 'of course you would' is a beautifully calibrated put-down — perfectly polite while absolutely withering. The joke is split between Ilana's guileless celebrity worship and the professional's barely-concealed disdain. Solid fish-out-of-water beat.

jokes.text: The maître d' admits he freaks out when celebrities come in — 'That's why I love my job'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks if he freaks out about celebrities, expecting to bond with him as a fellow fan
jokes.punchline: He confirms he absolutely does, with complete sincerity — undermining the dignified professional front he's maintained
jokes.explanation: A small subversion of the stuffy maître d' archetype — his candid celebrity fandom humanizes and deflates him simultaneously. Not a standout joke but serves the scene's rhythm well.

jokes.text: The Beyoncé/Jay-Z role assignment — 'Beyoncé and I do have similar curves'
jokes.setup: They're deciding who is Beyoncé and who is Jay-Z at their table
jokes.punchline: Ilana reasons through it: Abbi might want to be a dude, but also Ilana claims comparable curves to Beyoncé — delivered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: Ilana's self-image is always a rich vein for the show. Claiming Beyoncé-comparable curves is a solid expression of her unshakeable self-confidence. Light but charming.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana responding to 'do you have any food allergies' with general opinions about peanuts and gluten
jokes.setup: Server asks the standard allergy question
jokes.punchline: Instead of answering, they deliver unsolicited opinions on peanut allergies being fatal and gluten being 'an adult human thing across the board' — John has to clarify he meant their specific allergies
jokes.explanation: A beautifully observed joke about how people confuse 'do you have allergies' with an invitation to editorialize about allergies they've heard of. The 'gluten, which I think is like an adult human thing across the board' is a sharp skewer of millennial food discourse. John's clarification is the perfect straight-man button. Foreshadows Ilana's shellfish allergy reveal with devastating dramatic irony — on rewatch this is much funnier.

jokes.text: John the waiter asks about food allergies; Abbi launches into generic peanut allergy advice, then Ilana says 'Gluten, which I think is like an adult human thing across the board'
jokes.setup: John asks the standard server question about food allergies
jokes.punchline: Neither answers about themselves — Abbi gives public-health advice about peanuts, Ilana opines that all humans have gluten sensitivity; John clarifies he meant their personal allergies
jokes.explanation: Strong setup for the episode's main plot (Ilana IS allergic to shellfish, which she refuses to disclose). The gluten line is very Ilana — confident misinformation delivered as health wisdom. John's clarifying beat ('I actually meant if you have any food allergies...') is a great straight-man reset. The rewatch bonus is significant — knowing Ilana is hiding a shellfish allergy retroactively makes her evasiveness here funnier.

jokes.text: 'No, none that I, uh, want to share with you.' — Ilana's response to the allergy question
jokes.setup: John has clarified he wants to know about their specific allergies
jokes.punchline: Ilana says she has allergies but simply declines to share them — framed as a privacy matter rather than a safety one. This is the actual shellfish allergy setup.
jokes.explanation: On first watch this is a funny non-sequitur — why would you not want to share allergy information with your server? It reads as Ilana's contrarian weirdness. On rewatch, knowing she's severely allergic to the shellfish prix fixe they're about to eat, it becomes a devastating joke. The economy is perfect — seven words that contain an entire character arc. The 'want to share' framing (as if allergies are gossip) is brilliantly specific to Ilana's worldview of bodily autonomy extending even to medical necessity. Earns 8.2 primarily on structure, character integration, and rewatch value.

jokes.text: 'We like to say here that ramp is the truffle of onions. It's a dumb little joke. / We like to say that, too.'
jokes.setup: John delivers what is clearly a rehearsed restaurant in-joke about ramps
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately co-opts his catchphrase, claiming 'we like to say that, too' — a completely false statement that's also a bizarre power move
jokes.explanation: Ilana's social mimicry manifesting as aggressive appropriation of a stranger's catchphrase is a very specific and funny behavior. 'We like to say that, too' is absurd — they've never heard this joke before — but Ilana commits fully. Works as cringe comedy and character comedy simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'No, we are poppin' our cherry.'
jokes.setup: John asks if they've been to Octavia before
jokes.punchline: Ilana responds with 'poppin' our cherry' — a spectacularly inappropriate phrase for this context, delivered with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Functional fish-out-of-water joke — Ilana's vocabulary is constitutionally incapable of matching the restaurant's register. Not the sharpest version of this joke but serves the scene.

jokes.text: 'You just let me do the driving' — John's response, followed by his French send-off
jokes.setup: John is trying to maintain professional composure through Ilana's chaos
jokes.punchline: He adopts a slightly too-eager tour-guide energy ('let me do the driving'), then signs off with 'je m'appelle John, and bonne anniversaire' — a bizarre bilingual formality
jokes.explanation: John's character is a reliable comedic straight-man with his own slightly absurd pretensions. 'Je m'appelle John' is a funny little affectation — he introduces himself in French after already introducing himself in English. Light but functional.

jokes.text: 'He's tall and he's bilingual? / I'm short and I can't speak English good.'
jokes.setup: They're impressed by their attractive, French-speaking waiter
jokes.punchline: Abbi's self-deprecating 'I'm short and I can't speak English good' — the grammatical error proving her point mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: The self-undermining grammar in 'can't speak English good' is doing double duty — it's both the joke and the proof. The structure is tight and economical. The contrast with John's bilingual polish makes it land harder. Strong quotability because the joke lives in a single seven-word sentence that contains its own demonstration.

jokes.text: Ilana speculates John 'probably has a French press and one of those things that in the bathroom you clean your butthole with'
jokes.setup: They're admiring John's sophistication
jokes.punchline: Ilana's shorthand for 'classy man' includes a bidet, which she cannot name and describes via function — then Abbi has to supply 'bidet' twice because Ilana won't retain the word
jokes.explanation: The joke is less about the bidet and more about Ilana knowing what it is and does but having never bothered to learn its name. 'The thing that in the bathroom you clean your butthole with' as a circumlocution for sophistication is a specific and funny character choice. Abbi having to say 'bidet' twice suggests Ilana still doesn't absorb it, which is a nice little grace note.

jokes.text: The overheard kitchen argument — coworkers fighting about Anton, the sister who's an 'ex-junkie,' a stolen laptop, and bedbugs
jokes.setup: The elegant restaurant scene is interrupted by loud backstage bickering
jokes.punchline: The specificity escalates beautifully: sister needs picking up → can't be unsupervised → stole a laptop → 'I'm not sure she gave us bedbugs FOR SURE but I'm pretty damn positive'
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the restaurant's studied elegance and this sprawling, chaotic domestic dispute is the engine of the joke. The bedbug revelation lands particularly well because of its slightly hedged delivery — 'I'm not sure she gave us bedbugs FOR SURE but I'm pretty damn positive.' It punctures fine-dining pretension by reminding us that behind the curtain is just regular human chaos. First of a running backstage-argument gag.

jokes.text: 'Nose, vagina, butthole. If God didn't want us to put our fingers in there, then why did She make them perfectly finger-sized?'
jokes.setup: The conversation has drifted during the amuse-bouche; Ilana is apparently mid-thought on finger-related philosophy
jokes.punchline: The 'She' for God is a quiet feminist aside, but the main joke is the impeccable three-item list (nose, vagina, butthole) and the Intelligent Design-adjacent logic applied to finger-insertion
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is a parody of Intelligent Design / teleological argument applied to something maximally irreverent. 'If God didn't want us to put our fingers in there, then why did She make them perfectly finger-sized?' is genuinely clever — it mirrors the 'everything is designed for a purpose' argument structure exactly. The list of three is perfectly ordered (escalating intimacy). The female pronoun for God lands as a throwaway that does real character work. The 'exactly, that's what they're for' followup from Abbi as a straight-faced endorsement completes it. Earns 8.5 craft and 9.0 impact.

jokes.text: Ilana toasts Doug 'for turning you out' after Abbi reports slightly above-average sex
jokes.setup: Abbi says she had 'slightly above-average sex last night, which for me is incredible sex'
jokes.punchline: Ilana toasts 'to Doug, for turning you out' — using drug-dealing/pimping slang for a mediocre sexual experience, with Abbi's total acceptance of the framing
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: 'slightly above average' being framed as transformative, and the language mismatch of 'turned you out' applied to what is clearly a B-minus sexual experience. Abbi's 'for me, you know, is incredible sex' self-qualifier is a sharp piece of character writing about her particular romantic situation.

jokes.text: Abbi planned sex to happen 'at the stroke of midnight' as she turned 26 — the birthday sex ritual
jokes.setup: Ilana calls it a 'spiritual sexual experience'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's sincere response — 'I just feel like really good inside' — which she means literally (spiritually) but also happens to be literally true (the condom is still in there, as we learn later)
jokes.explanation: Scores higher on rewatch — 'I just feel really good inside' is a perfect accidental double entendre given what we learn about the lost condom. On first watch it's just a sweet, slightly overwrought moment. On rewatch it's a perfectly planted gag. The midnight birthday sex planning is also a nice character beat — Abbi's romantic punctiliousness contrasted with the messy reality.

jokes.text: 'Joie de... jooge' — Abbi's failed French with the waiter
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to thank John in sophisticated, French-inflected language ('joie de vivre')
jokes.punchline: She trails off into 'jooge' — a sound that is neither French nor English, suggesting she started a phrase she couldn't finish
jokes.explanation: A small, perfect moment. 'Jooge' as a non-word standing in for abandoned sophistication is exactly the right kind of Abbi awkwardness — she's trying harder than Ilana to perform posh but keeps tripping. The callback potential to 'can't speak English good' is implicit.

jokes.text: 'I just peed out a condom.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has gone to the restroom; there's a beat of restaurant ambiance
jokes.punchline: She returns and delivers this flat announcement — the most extreme possible thing she could say, delivered with the shell-shocked directness of someone still processing
jokes.explanation: The funniest line in the episode and one of the better Broad City punchlines. The economy is a perfect 10 — six words that contain maximum information and maximum comic impact. The joke is earned by the buildup: we know Abbi had good sex, we know she feels 'really good inside,' and this retroactive reveal reframes everything. The flat, declarative delivery (no exclamation, no hysteria — she's past hysteria) is essential to why it lands. The scene cut after the bathroom ambiance makes the silence before 'I just peed out a condom' part of the joke. Earns 8.7 craft.

jokes.text: 'Did you pee it or poop it?' — Ilana's immediate follow-up question
jokes.setup: Abbi has just said she peed out a condom
jokes.punchline: Ilana's first instinct is to establish the exit route — 'pee or poop?' — with the same procedural urgency she'd use for anything else, followed by Abbi's affronted 'Why'd you immediately go there?'
jokes.explanation: Ilana's response is so specifically her — no shock, no sympathy, immediate forensic curiosity about the mechanism. 'Did you pee it or poop it?' followed by Abbi's 'Why'd you immediately go there? I didn't poop it, dude' is a perfect two-hander. Ilana's follow-up 'Hey, it's 2014. Anal's on the menu' is a beautiful escalation that treats backdoor biology as a matter of contemporary social currency. Character integration earns the 9.0 — only Ilana asks this question this way.

jokes.text: 'Hey, it's 2014. Anal's on the menu.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is offended that Ilana assumed poop-exit was plausible
jokes.punchline: Ilana defends the assumption by gesturing at the cultural moment — as if anal sex becoming more mainstream means assuming it is simply reasonable inference
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable. The joke treats a sexual practice as a year-specific cultural development — the same way you'd say 'it's 2014, everyone has a smartphone.' Ilana's deployment of cultural currency to normalize the assumption is perfectly her. Strong economy, strong quotability.

jokes.text: 'Everything I've done has happened to you.' / 'Not yet is what I meant to say.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if peeing out a condom has happened to Ilana; Ilana says no
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately revises to 'Not yet is what I meant to say' — reframing her denial as a timeline issue rather than an experience gap, implying she considers this inevitable
jokes.explanation: The line 'everything I've done has happened to you' is a beautiful encapsulation of their friendship dynamic — Ilana as the initiating chaos agent whose experiences eventually transfer to Abbi. The self-correction to 'not yet' flips the script briefly (suggesting Abbi is doing things Ilana hasn't) then reinstates Ilana's supremacy by framing it as only a matter of time. Tight structure.

jokes.text: 'So what? You're a nasty bitch, who cares?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is embarrassed/distressed about the condom incident
jokes.punchline: Ilana's comfort-giving is the world's most bracing pep talk: 'you're a nasty bitch, who cares?' delivered as affectionate solidarity
jokes.explanation: The joke is that this is meant entirely as support. 'Nasty bitch' in Ilana's vocabulary is unambiguously a compliment — a term of admiration and solidarity. The recontextualization of 'nasty' as a positive identity is core to the show's ethos, and this is a clean expression of it. Highly quotable as a friend-comfort statement.

jokes.text: Ilana asks the busboy to guard their clams while they go get high
jokes.setup: They're leaving the table mid-course to smoke weed
jokes.punchline: Ilana's only concern is protecting her expensive seafood; she treats the busboy as a sentinel for this mission
jokes.explanation: Functional scene beat — Ilana's food-possessiveness is a running joke and this is a clean expression of it. The clam-protection mission is also a nice setup for the 'they left your clams' payoff later.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, well, I shouldn't have tried to throw myself a surprise party. / You should have tried. It was a mistake.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says this year is a 'maje improvement' from last year's birthday
jokes.punchline: 'I shouldn't have tried to throw myself a surprise party' — but Ilana's response makes it worse by affirming the attempt while calling it a mistake
jokes.explanation: Strong joke. 'I shouldn't have tried to throw myself a surprise party' is inherently funny as a premise — the self-surprise party requires you to simultaneously be the planner and the surprisee, which is structurally impossible. Ilana's response is the ideal follow-up: 'You should have tried. It was a mistake.' — she validates the attempt while condemning the execution, which is both supportive and devastating. The line 'You should have tried' as a standalone compliment that immediately becomes a condemnation is tight writing.

jokes.text: Ilana offers a hit to the busboy, who declines and then awkwardly thanks them 'for the food' he didn't prepare
jokes.setup: They're smoking on the fire escape/outside; Ilana offers the busboy a hit
jokes.punchline: The busboy thanks them for the food but then clarifies 'I don't prepare the food. I just, uh, clear the plates.' Abbi says 'Yeah, but... you know.' They've given him the credit for someone else's labor, and his correction just makes it more awkward
jokes.explanation: A quietly charming awkward beat. Abbi's stoned 'Yeah, but... you know' after the busboy has explicitly clarified he didn't make the food is a perfect approximation of marijuana-softened logic — she means something by it, she just can't access what. The subsequent 'you know what I've been thinking about' pivot into the dead animals monologue suggests the weed is fully operational.

jokes.text: Abbi's stoned realization: 'Every animal from every movie we've loved as a kid — probably dead.'
jokes.setup: They're high on the fire escape, conversation drifting
jokes.punchline: The existential inventory: Air Bud — dead. All 101 Dalmatians probably dead. Babe — maybe not (pigs live longer). Tramp — cartoon, Abbi knew that. The list is funny both as stoner logic and as a precise calibration of animal lifespans vs. film release dates
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully executed stoner logic sequence. The premise is actually correct — Air Bud is definitely dead — which makes it funnier. 'We could have eaten Babe tonight' is a dark detour. The pig-lives-longer-than-dogs correction that redirects to turtles is the kind of half-right fact-checking that characterizes this type of conversation. The Tramp coda ('Tramp's a cartoon' / 'I know that' / dramatic pause / 'She was for real. I know that it was a cartoon.') is a perfect depiction of stoned self-defense of a position you've just abandoned. The 'Tramp was my first sexual crush' button is a natural escalation.

jokes.text: 'I know that. She was for real. I know that it was a cartoon.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just included Tramp (a cartoon dog) in her list of actually-dead movie animals
jokes.punchline: The busboy points out Tramp is a cartoon; Abbi doubles down before collapsing — 'I know that. She was for real. I know that it was a cartoon.' — three consecutive contradictory statements
jokes.explanation: The implosion of Abbi's stoned self-assurance across three consecutive self-contradictory sentences is beautiful. 'I know that. She was for real. I know that it was a cartoon.' — she starts defending, pivots to doubling down, then admits defeat, all in about seven words. The structural collapse mirrors actual stoned backpedaling perfectly.

jokes.text: 'You know, that Tramp was my first sexual crush. He was hot. He was like a spunky bad boy.'
jokes.setup: They've established Tramp is a cartoon; conversation is winding down with the busboy
jokes.punchline: Abbi reveals her first sexual crush was a cartoon dog, described with the vocabulary of a real human attraction: 'spunky bad boy'
jokes.explanation: Having just definitively established Tramp is a cartoon, Abbi immediately pivots to sexualizing him. The 'spunky bad boy' taxonomy applied to an animated Italian restaurant stray is funny and very Abbi — her earnest romanticism is the joke. Sets up Ilana's Stimpy reveal as a callback.

jokes.text: 'You know who was mine? Stimpy. Stimpy from 'Ren and Stimpy'? Yeah, I feel like he has a similar build to Lincoln.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has confessed her cartoon dog crush; Ilana has one to top it
jokes.punchline: Stimpy — and then the comparison to 'Lincoln' (presumably a current partner) is left completely unexamined, as though comparing a cartoon cat's physique to a real person's is normal
jokes.explanation: Ilana's choice of Stimpy is perfectly calibrated — Stimpy is the more passive, dopey, devoted one of the pair, which tells us something about Ilana's taste. But the pivot to 'he has a similar build to Lincoln' is the joke's peak — treating a cartoon cat's body type as a meaningful point of physical comparison for a real human being, completely casually. Abbi's 'Huh' is the perfect reaction beat.

jokes.text: 'They left your clams. The world is good, dude.'
jokes.setup: They've returned from getting high; the guarded clams are waiting
jokes.punchline: The mission was successful, and Ilana's relief — 'The world is good, dude' — treats the preservation of an appetizer as a philosophical resolution
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback button. 'The world is good, dude' as a response to clams not being cleared is a beautiful deflation of profundity — Ilana's metric for cosmic okayness is whether her expensive seafood is still on the table. Economy of the payoff is strong.

jokes.text: Ilana's face is red and her eye is 'blowing up' from the shellfish allergy — revealed immediately after they've been eating nothing but shellfish
jokes.setup: Ilana refused to disclose her allergy (joke 12); the prix fixe is all shellfish (established at ordering)
jokes.punchline: Abbi notices Ilana's face is swelling shut from anaphylaxis after multiple courses — the long-fuse setup from the allergy question finally detonates
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of the allergy setup is very well-earned. The joke isn't just 'she's allergic and she ate shellfish' — it's that this was entirely preventable (she was asked directly), she knew, and she calculated it was worth it. 'I really know my body, dude. I go up to the edge and then I scale it back.' delivered while her face is visibly swelling is peak Ilana self-mythology meeting physical reality. The episode's construction earns this moment its 8.5 craft score — it's a 10-minute payoff on a 10-second setup.

jokes.text: 'I really know my body, dude. I go up to the edge and then I scale it back. I'm good at it, honestly.'
jokes.setup: Her face is visibly swelling from anaphylaxis
jokes.punchline: She describes her shellfish-tolerance strategy as a refined skill while the visual evidence directly contradicts every word
jokes.explanation: Character integration earns the 9.5 — no one else on television would frame anaphylactic shock as a skill they possess. 'I go up to the edge and then I scale it back' as a philosophy applied to a potentially fatal allergic reaction is a perfect distillation of Ilana's manic self-confidence. The joke lives entirely in the gap between her claimed expertise and the visual reality of her face. Earns 8.4.

jokes.text: 'How do I not know you're allergic to shellfish? / You don't know everything about me. / You text me every time you take a dump. I know about the pimple on your nipple and I'm, like, the holder of your Social Security card.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is incredulous that this never came up
jokes.punchline: Ilana insists on having 'mystery' — Abbi's rebuttal catalogs the intimate knowledge she already has, each item more invasive than the last, culminating in 'holder of your Social Security card'
jokes.explanation: The escalating inventory of intimacy is brilliantly constructed. 'You text me every time you take a dump' → 'I know about the pimple on your nipple' → 'I'm the holder of your Social Security card' — this is a perfect three-beat escalation where each item is more absurd than the last. The Social Security card as the apex of intimacy (stranger than the bodily oversharing) is the right joke. Ilana's response — 'I have mystery, too. I haven't even told you yet that I want to put my fist in our waiter's mouth sexually' — is a perfect definition of what Ilana considers private information.

jokes.text: 'I have mystery, too. I haven't even told you yet that I want to put my fist in our waiter's mouth sexually.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she knows everything about Ilana; Ilana insists she has secrets
jokes.punchline: Ilana's proof of mystery is a revelation that is somehow both too much information AND confirms that her withheld information is exclusively sexual/bizarre
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Ilana's definition of 'mystery' is 'things I haven't told you yet' rather than 'things I won't tell you' — the disclosure of the fist/mouth thing proves she has no real secrets, just a backlog of oversharing. 'Sexually' as a clarifying addendum (as opposed to — what? A non-sexual fist in the mouth?) is perfect. 'I feel like he only has sex on top of baby grands' is a coda that proves the John-as-class-object bit has been running in Ilana's head this whole time. High character integration.

jokes.text: 'I feel like he only has sex on top of baby grands.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing their waiter John's sexual style/habits
jokes.punchline: The specific image of baby grand pianos as sexual surfaces is a very precise piece of aspirational class-eroticism — it's not just that he's fancy, it's that his sex involves furniture that costs more than their apartments
jokes.explanation: Seven words that contain a complete and vivid comic universe. 'Baby grands' (not grand pianos — baby grands specifically) is doing enormous work: it's precise, impractical, aspirational, and slightly absurd. The joke collapses the distance between class aspiration and sexuality in a way that's uniquely Ilana. Highly quotable and highly economical. Earns 8.1.

jokes.text: Second overheard kitchen argument: cable/Homeland, 'you gross baby'
jokes.setup: The backstage argument continues as a counterpoint to the elegant dining room
jokes.punchline: 'I'll cancel the cable, then have fun figuring out what happens on Homeland, you gross baby' — the escalation of stakes from family drama to TV spoilerage as the ultimate threat
jokes.explanation: The 'Homeland' cable threat is a very specific and timely (2014) escalation — fine-dining pretension shattered by a streaming/cable dispute. 'You gross baby' as a final insult is satisfying. The running kitchen argument gag works because each installment raises absurdity slightly.

jokes.text: John shouts orders mid-fight: 'I need a 42. I need an onion ring, I need a tagliatelle with light sauce.'
jokes.setup: The kitchen argument is at a fever pitch
jokes.punchline: John pivots mid-screaming fight to shout restaurant orders in the same register, then presumably continues fighting
jokes.explanation: A good visual/tonal gag — the fight and the professional kitchen operation occupying the same emotional register. Works better as a visual moment than on the page.

jokes.text: The crab three ways, lobster nine ways reveal — 'Get in my belly!'
jokes.setup: John announces a seafood medley as Ilana's face swells shut
jokes.punchline: The medley is the most shellfish-dense course yet (crab THREE ways, lobster NINE ways), and Ilana responds with 'Get in my belly!' — Austin Powers enthusiasm for the food that is actively killing her
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'nine ways' for the lobster is an escalating absurdity in itself. 'Get in my belly!' is a pop-culture drop but earns its spot because the timing is perfect — it's the most wrong thing to say in this moment. The escalation of shellfish serving methods vs. the escalation of anaphylaxis is a tightly constructed visual gag/escalation hybrid.

jokes.text: Abbi's singing confidence — 'I know I can't really sing. But if I had private lessons and learned to breathe, I could sing really well.'
jokes.setup: Conversation has drifted; Abbi shares a musical aspiration
jokes.punchline: The self-awareness of 'I know I can't really sing' immediately undercut by 'but with lessons and breathing I could sing really well' — the classic Dunning-Kruger qualification
jokes.explanation: A relatable self-delusion joke done with enough specificity ('learned to really breathe') to elevate it above the generic version. The 'but don't repeat that to anybody' request — as if this is classified information — adds a nice layer.

jokes.text: Abbi calls Doug from the bathroom to tell him she peed out a condom — he reveals he didn't use a condom
jokes.setup: Abbi tells Doug she peed out a condom at a fancy birthday dinner
jokes.punchline: 'We didn't use a condom last night' — the condom has been in her body since a different encounter (boring Ben, four days ago)
jokes.explanation: The structural escalation here is excellent — the initial joke (peed out a condom) becomes a bigger joke (it wasn't from the sex she thought). The callback to 'boring Ben' recontextualizes the timeline, and '...which means there was a condom in my person for four days' is the episode's second-best flat delivery line. The escalation from embarrassment to potential medical/sexual health crisis to 'four days' is well-paced.

jokes.text: 'Because, like a lady, I keep my eyes closed when I make love.'
jokes.setup: Doug says he took the condom off mid-act; Abbi didn't notice
jokes.punchline: Abbi's explanation for not seeing him remove it — 'like a lady, I keep my eyes closed when I make love' — is a hilarious piece of self-described Victorian propriety from a woman who just peed out a four-day-old condom
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and sharply written. The irony is dense: 'like a lady' is an absurd self-description for someone in this situation; 'make love' as a euphemism used by the woman who peed out a condom is tonally perfect. The joke articulates Abbi's specific character (romantic, slightly self-deceiving about her own sexuality) in a way only she could deliver. Earns 8.1 on character integration, economy, and quotability.

jokes.text: 'You're a condom girl. I will make a note of that for next time.' — Doug's response
jokes.setup: Abbi explains she always uses condoms
jokes.punchline: 'You're a condom girl. I will make a note of that for next time' — a spectacularly tone-deaf response from a man who just caused a medical incident
jokes.explanation: 'I will make a note of that for next time' — the clinical, managerial phrasing of filing away a sexual preference after an incident like this is both funny and character-revealing about Doug. The joke also sets up Abbi's 'there's not gonna be a next time' / '...are you sure, Abbi?' exchange.

jokes.text: 'No, I'm not sure, because I'm not sure about anything. Something just fell out of my body, so I gotta go deal with that, and you need to deal with me dealing with that.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says there won't be a next time; Doug challenges 'are you sure?'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's philosophical uncertainty speech — 'I'm not sure about anything' — grounded by the very specific 'something just fell out of my body' as the epistemological trigger for her uncertainty
jokes.explanation: 'You need to deal with me dealing with that' is a very good, very quotable line — the meta-demand that her partner acknowledge her processing time rather than resolve the thing itself. The grounding of existential uncertainty in 'something just fell out of my body' is funny and very Abbi.

jokes.text: Seafood turducken — small clam inside the mouth of a lobster inside a larger clam — 'Suck on those legs a little longer'
jokes.setup: John announces increasingly absurd seafood architecture as Ilana's face deteriorates
jokes.punchline: The turducken concept applied to shellfish — a clam inside a lobster inside a clam — reaches peak absurdity, and Ilana's 'I'm gonna suck on those legs a little longer' is a commitment bit on her part
jokes.explanation: The seafood turducken is a beautiful absurdist escalation of the 'crab three ways / lobster nine ways' earlier. John's increasingly baroque presentations are a running gag that contrasts restaurant pretension with the catastrophe unfolding at the table. Ilana sucking on lobster legs while in anaphylaxis is peak commitment humor.

jokes.text: 'I could still wink, see?' — followed by the wink (presumably horrifying given facial swelling)
jokes.setup: Abbi says Ilana's face looks insane; Ilana says it's not that bad
jokes.punchline: The wink, performed with a face that is clearly in terrible shape, as proof of functionality
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that must be seen to be fully appreciated but reads clearly from transcript context. The beat after the wink is described as 'Yeah, this is pretty bad' — the wink evidently did not support Ilana's case. Using a wink as proof of face-functionality when your face is swollen shut is a very specific and funny choice.

jokes.text: 'Your face looks like the underbelly of a tugboat.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to make Ilana admit how bad her allergic reaction is
jokes.punchline: The specific simile — not just 'bad' or 'swollen' but 'the underbelly of a tugboat' — is a very precise and unexpected comparison
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable lines in the episode. The simile works because of its specificity — not 'a boat' but 'the underbelly of a tugboat' specifically. Tugboats are particularly barnacled, rust-streaked, and rough-textured. The precision is what makes it funny rather than just mean. Earns 8.1 on originality, economy, and quotability.

jokes.text: Ilana has an epi pen in her clutch — she planned for Abbi to administer it
jokes.setup: Abbi says she needs an epi pen; Ilana says it's in her clutch
jokes.punchline: Ilana not only brought an epi pen — she planned to be stabbed with it at some point in the evening, as part of her 'knowing my limit' strategy. 'So you planned on having me stab you with an epi pen?' / 'You know, stop trying to change the subject.'
jokes.explanation: This is the perfect culmination of the allergy arc. Ilana didn't just forget to mention her allergy — she engineered this situation, brought emergency medical equipment, and assigned Abbi a medical role without informing her. 'Stop trying to change the subject' as a deflection from 'you planned to have me stab you' is a character-integration 9.5 moment — the deflection IS the character. The rewatch value is high because you can now see the whole evening was planned this way. Earns 8.6.

jokes.text: 'There was a condom in my person for four days.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just done the math on when the condom was left behind
jokes.punchline: The flat delivery of 'in my person for four days' — 'my person' as a formal way of saying 'my vagina' — is the joke
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect. 'In my person' is a perfectly chosen euphemism — formal, slightly legal-sounding, treating her own body as a sovereign entity. 'Four days' lands as a number with comic weight. The flat delivery mirrors 'I just peed out a condom' (the original reveal) in tone, which is good structural symmetry. Earns 8.2.

jokes.text: The epi pen chaos — Ilana grabs it back, Abbi takes it, Ilana keeps eating ('two more bites, it is so expensive'), Abbi stabs herself with it
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to administer the epi pen; Ilana keeps eating and resisting
jokes.punchline: In the struggle, Abbi stabs herself with the epi pen — she is now the one who has injected epinephrine
jokes.explanation: The culmination of the physical escalation. The episode builds carefully to this moment — the allergic reaction, the planned epi pen, Ilana's insistence on eating — and resolves it with maximum chaos. Ilana's 'two more bites, it is so expensive' while resisting medical treatment is the perfect character note. The accidental self-stab is earned physical comedy rather than random slapstick because it results from Abbi's very specific attempts to help someone who doesn't want to be helped. The structure earns the 8.0 craft; the moment earns 9.0 impact.

jokes.text: 'Whoo! Adrenaline! I... could actually take that pen. I feel so alive right now!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has accidentally stabbed herself with an epi pen
jokes.punchline: The adrenaline rush from the self-injection turns Abbi manic — 'I feel so alive right now!' — then she claims she could 'actually take' the pen (she's now on board with being stabbed)
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Abbi going from reluctant administrator to enthusiastic self-stabber — is a good escalation beat. 'I feel so alive right now' is a funny medical side-effect expressed as a personal revelation. The physical energy shift implied by the whoop is a strong visual/performance moment.

jokes.text: 'It's my birthday, I'm the king of the world!' — Abbi on adrenaline while Ilana calls 911
jokes.setup: Abbi is rushing through the restaurant on an adrenaline high; Ilana is trying to call an ambulance
jokes.punchline: The Titanic reference, the juxtaposition of birthday celebration and medical emergency, and the two-cake-to-go request ('we'll take those two molten lava cakes to go') in the middle of an ambulance call
jokes.explanation: The chaos of this sequence lands well. The molten lava cake callback ('they said it was life-changing' — and now they're going to the hospital) is a nice structural circle. 'I'm the king of the world' on adrenaline at 26 is a good absurdist image. Ilana trying to call 911 while Abbi zooms around is a solid physical comedy beat.

jokes.text: Abbi, adrenaline-spiked, calls for the ambulance while Ilana shouts 'It's my birthday, I'm the king of the world!'
jokes.setup: The restaurant is now in full chaos; someone is calling 911
jokes.punchline: Ilana's self-declared birthday kingship (quoting Titanic) juxtaposed with Abbi calmly calling for an ambulance
jokes.explanation: The chaos peak of the episode — Ilana is triumphant ('king of the world') while Abbi is practically managing an emergency. A great juxtaposition of their different responses to the same situation.

jokes.text: 'We'll take those two molten lava cakes to go.'
jokes.setup: They are in the middle of a medical emergency / ambulance call
jokes.punchline: The birthday lava cake — established in the episode's first restaurant beat as the evening's pinnacle — is claimed on the way out to the ambulance
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most satisfying callbacks. The lava cake was Abbi's birthday benchmark — the whole meal was leading to it — and it's claimed in the midst of anaphylaxis and an ambulance call. The economy is near-perfect: five words that resolve a 14-minute setup. The timing (mid-911-call) is ideal. Earns 8.0.

jokes.text: The kitchen argument triple-cross during the 911 call: 'Why don't you have the ambulance drop you off at a mental hospital... I hope you die in your sleep... I am gonna die because you're killing me slowly... Speak clearly, someone is dying'
jokes.setup: Ilana is trying to call an ambulance while the kitchen fight reaches peak chaos
jokes.punchline: The kitchen couple's insults to each other ('I hope you die in your sleep') overlap with Ilana's actual medical emergency call ('someone is dying'), creating maximum confusion about who is actually dying
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the running kitchen argument gag is well-constructed: the overheard bickering has been building, and it erupts at the worst possible moment during an actual medical emergency call. 'I hope you die in your sleep' immediately followed by the paramedic-call context where someone actually might die is sharp structural comedy. 'Speak clearly, someone is dying' lands as both Ilana addressing the dispatcher and a commentary on the entire situation.

jokes.text: 'Abbi, you are the love of my... life.' — Ilana to Abbi on the ambulance gurney
jokes.setup: Ilana is being loaded onto an ambulance; Abbi is holding her
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'life' — the ellipsis suggesting Ilana almost said something else (presumably something more sexually charged) before correcting to the platonic declaration
jokes.explanation: The pause is doing all the work. The show's central relationship is a friendship that constantly bumps against romantic language without quite tipping over, and 'love of my... life' captures that perfectly. The ellipsis suggests self-editing, which implies the original word was more. Character integration earns the 8.5 — this is the show's central emotional note, played in the middle of a medical emergency, which is very Broad City.

jokes.text: The hospital bucket list — 'own an elliptical / go to a pug farm / do heroin under the aurora borealis'
jokes.setup: They're recovering in the hospital, talking about their bucket list
jokes.punchline: The bucket list escalates from mundane ('own an elliptical') to whimsical ('lay with a lot of pugs') to specific-drug-tourism ('do heroin under the aurora borealis') — the last item delivered with equal earnestness
jokes.explanation: The three-item escalation is perfectly calibrated: elliptical (mundane aspiration), pug farm (gently whimsical), heroin under the aurora borealis (specifically depraved tourism). The specificity of the last item — not just 'do heroin' but 'under the aurora borealis' — is what makes it exceptional. It suggests they've thought carefully about the optimal heroin-consumption conditions. 'So none of those can go. Good luck editing.' from Abbi is a strong button.

jokes.text: Ilana's bucket list: 'make your own Passover Seder / to be able to squirt / to be held in Abbi's arms'
jokes.setup: They're reading through bucket list items
jokes.punchline: The three-item list moves from culturally appropriate (Passover Seder) to physical aspiration ('to be able to squirt') to sincere emotional longing ('to be held in Abbi's arms') — and the last item has just been accomplished
jokes.explanation: The list structure mirrors the previous bucket list's escalation but inverts it — ending on the most sincere and tender item rather than the most depraved. The reveal that 'to be held in Abbi's arms' was a genuine bucket list item, combined with its being checked off during the emergency, is both funny and genuinely sweet. The Passover Seder as a casual bucket list item (not a meaningful spiritual goal — just something she wants to try making) and 'to be able to squirt' as a self-improvement goal placed between them is excellent three-item construction. Earns 8.4.

jokes.text: The hospital hallway ER crisis playing out in the background as Abbi and Ilana continue their conversation, ending with 'Time of death, 2:47 AM'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are having a warm, funny post-crisis conversation in the hospital; ER drama unfolds behind them
jokes.punchline: 'Time of death, 2:47 AM' — someone dies in the background while they're talking about bucket lists; they don't react
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy background gag — a patient dies in the background during their cheerful chat. The indifference is the joke. 'Time of death' announced casually while they discuss pugs and ellipticals is a nice tonal counterpoint. The earlier kitchen argument 'I hope you die in your sleep' has a grim echo here.

jokes.text: The hospital ER scene — doctor dialog about epinephrine, 'patient not responsive,' time of death 2:47 AM — a totally different patient dies while they're talking
jokes.setup: Medical drama language fills the scene — they're in the ER
jokes.punchline: The medical urgency of the ER scene turns out to be about someone completely unrelated — Mr. Rosenberg — who dies while Abbi and Ilana are having their heart-to-heart conversation in adjacent curtains
jokes.explanation: The misdirect is effective — the ER drama sounds like it's about Ilana, then we realize it's a parallel patient crisis. The comedy of Abbi and Ilana's warm conversation happening right next to someone dying is a dark, effective tonal contrast. 'Time of death, 2:47 AM' as a joke button is a good dark comedy beat.

jokes.text: 'You know what another one of mine is? To be an Asian girl.'
jokes.setup: They're continuing the bucket list conversation post-ER drama
jokes.punchline: Ilana's bucket list includes changing her racial identity — not as a social observation, but as a genuine desire to experience being an Asian girl
jokes.explanation: Ilana's race-swap aspiration is characteristically her — she approaches race with a kind of intersectional tourist curiosity that is the show's particular register. The follow-up 'if technology ever allows for it, I'd love a crack at every race' is the key escalation — she wants to sample all racial experiences, framed as a technological limitation rather than a conceptual one. Moderately sharp.

jokes.text: 'First night of my 26th year — pee out a condom, go to the hospital. I think that means this year is just gonna be really— / Fun and cool and fresh? / I was gonna say awesome, but yeah.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to find meaning in the evening's catastrophes
jokes.punchline: The gap between Abbi's intended 'awesome' and Ilana's 'fun and cool and fresh?' — Ilana's word choices are oddly specific and strangely anticlimactic compared to the night they've had
jokes.explanation: A functional birthday-theme button. 'Pee out a condom, go to the hospital' as a two-item summary of the evening is good economy. Ilana's 'fun and cool and fresh' as a descriptor for a year that began with medical trauma is amusingly inadequate.

jokes.text: Ilana's condom policy: 'From now on, you will be sending me a picture of every dick that you are about to host wearing a condom.'
jokes.setup: Ilana imposes new 'strictest condom policies' on Abbi
jokes.punchline: The policy is: photograph every penis in a condom before intercourse and send it to Ilana for her records — 'host' as a hospitality-industry euphemism for the sexual act is the key word choice
jokes.explanation: 'Every dick that you are about to host' is an exceptional euphemism — 'host' reframes sex as hospitality, which is a specifically Ilana-esque verbal choice. The policy itself is funny because it's simultaneously invasive (Ilana will be receiving condom-penis photos) and protective (she genuinely cares about Abbi's safety). The 'Deal? No deal.' exchange is a fast, clean button. Earns 8.2 on character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Ilana's family's 'gross sex list' game — 'Who is the grossest person you still kind of want to have sex with?'
jokes.setup: Ilana reveals her mom's gross sex list starts with Howie Mandel
jokes.punchline: The game definition ('grossest person you still kind of want to have sex with') is framed as wholesome family entertainment, with Howie Mandel as the entry point
jokes.explanation: Howie Mandel as the opening ante for the gross sex list is a perfect choice — deeply specific, not obviously sexy, but somehow not impossible. The framing of it as a family game is the primary joke. The game definition itself is a good premise.

jokes.text: Abbi's gross sex list answer: O.J. Simpson
jokes.setup: Abbi has never played this game; she gives her first answer
jokes.punchline: O.J. — Ilana's response is the real joke: 'I have never even thought of a murderer' / 'Definitely O.J. / Oh my God!' — Ilana is floored because Abbi has unlocked a new dimension of the game in her first attempt
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the final sequence. Abbi's O.J. answer is funny on multiple levels: it's 'gross' for reasons that have nothing to do with appearance (he's a double murderer), it's historically grounded ('he was on the cover of magazines'), and it evolves the game's premises in a direction Ilana — who invented the game — had never considered. 'I have never even thought of a murderer' as the response of the game's creator to a first-time player is a perfect inversion. The shared enthusiasm ('He was gorgeous. He was a 10') is character-perfect. High impact because the room erupts when the O.J. reveal lands.

jokes.text: Ilana's newest entry: the Six Flags old man — and both women physically imitating his dancing in the hospital in heels
jokes.setup: Ilana has a new gross sex list entry — the old man from Six Flags commercials
jokes.punchline: Both women immediately break into impression of him, dancing wildly ('a lot faster') while Abbi marvels she can do it in heels — the physical comedy of two women in hospital doing the Six Flags dance ends the episode
jokes.explanation: A strong visual closing joke — the Six Flags old man is a very specific pop-culture reference that connects Ilana's gross sex list to a shared cultural artifact. The physical performance aspect (both women doing the dance in a hospital, one in heels, with 'but a lot faster') is a good episode button. Ends on physical comedy and friendship energy, which is tonally right for the show.

jokes.text: 'Hold up, though, dude. We've got to go back to O.J.'
jokes.setup: They've been riffing on the Six Flags guy; Ilana abruptly redirects
jokes.punchline: The O.J. answer was so good that Ilana can't let it go — 'we've got to go back to O.J.' as a final line suggests the conversation isn't over, and also that O.J. has earned a permanent place in the discussion
jokes.explanation: A perfect episode-closing callback. 'We've got to go back to O.J.' as a final line is economical, character-consistent, and leaves us in the middle of a conversation rather than resolving it — which is the correct Broad City closing energy. It also implicitly confirms that O.J. won the gross sex list definitively, which is a strong structural note on the entire sequence.
